original_abstract: Soccer is more than just the world’s most popular spectator sport; it is a profound social phenomenon that has shaped human culture since the late nineteenth century. Beyond its role as global entertainment, soccer serves as a unique lens through which scholars examine the complexities of modern life, including politics, economics, religion, media, and public health. This volume of Soccer & Society, the first international journal dedicated to the game, explores these dynamics within an increasingly globalized world. By integrating anthropological, historical, sociological, and aesthetic perspectives, the journal provides an interdisciplinary forum for understanding the evolving relationship between the "world game" and the communities it inhabits. Central to this discussion is the power of soccer to drive social good, foster inclusivity, and influence behavioral change. This issue specifically highlights how researchers and practitioners are leveraging the game's massive reach to address contemporary challenges in social welfare and corporate responsibility. We remain committed to fostering original empirical research from global scholars and establishing a truly international platform that reflects the sport's universal impact.
humanized_abstract: Soccer has been a world-wide phenomenon since the late nineteenth century. As the world's most popular sport, it is so much more than just a sporting event. The impact that soccer has on the world of social life has made it a focus for studies of modernity across a wide range of disciplines including politics, economics, religion, media and public health. This first issue of Soccer and Society, the first international journal on the sport, seeks to investigate the global reach of the world game and its impact on diverse social settings across the world. Bringing together anthropological, historical, sociological and aesthetic perspectives, the journal offers an interdisciplinary forum for discussing the complex and multifaceted relationship between the world's most popular sport and its different social settings. While the world game has a multitude of effects on diverse social settings, one major legacy of the global phenomenon of the beautiful game has been social good. Soccer has enabled the achievement of numerous social goods through a range of processes including social solidarity, the empowerment of marginalised groups and through social behaviour change initiatives. This issue explores the potential of soccer as a vehicle for research and intervention by examining the relationships between the world game and social welfare and responsible business practices. We aim to develop new scholarship from new authors on a global basis and establish a global network of scholars that is truly international in outlook and focus the world's attention on the many and diverse impacts of the world game.
